claim_text: We observe that all DFDs along with HFEN substantially outperform NRMSE, SSIM, and PSNR in terms of correlation with mean reader scores, similar to the findings of the Mason study6 of natural images.
manuscript_section: Discussion / Results (citing Mason 2020 as prior finding on IQM-reader correlations)

sub_claims.text: Mason 2020 found that several higher-performing IQMs substantially outperform RMSE, SSIM, and PSNR in correlation with radiologist (reader) scores.

evidence.snippet: When considering SROCC calculated from combining scores from all radiologists across all image types, RMSE and SSIM had lower SROCC than six of the other IQMs included in the study (VIF, FSIM, NQM, GMSD, IWSSIM, and HDRVDP). In no case did SSIM have a higher SROCC or significantly smaller residuals than RMSE.

Note that RMSE and SSIM are among the metrics with the lowest SROCC. Overall, VIF had the highest SROCC values... the sorted IQMs show that for many of the radiologists in the study, VIF, FSIM, and NQM perform statistically better than the other IQMs included in the study. SSIM did not perform statistically better than any another IQM including RMSE.
SSIM does not show a significantly stronger correlation with radiologist opinion of diagnostic image quality than RMSE, and that there are other objective IQMs that perform better.

After normalizing each radiologist's score and combining scores across all images, we found that VIF exhibited the highest SROCC of all the metrics evaluated in this study. These results suggest that VIF provides the most accurate surrogate measure of subjective image quality scores of a radiologist...

sub_claims.nuance: Mason 2020 evaluated 10 IQMs (RMSE, SSIM, PSNR, MSSSIM, IWSSIM, GMSD, FSIM, HDRVDP, NQM, VIF) and found VIF, FSIM, NQM, GMSD, IWSSIM, and HDRVDP outperform RMSE and SSIM by SROCC with expert radiologist scores; PSNR is monotonic with RMSE so it behaves similarly. This directly supports the manuscript's 'outperform NRMSE/SSIM/PSNR' pattern. Note: HFEN itself was NOT evaluated in Mason 2020 — the manuscript's 'similar to findings of Mason' refers to the same pattern (better IQMs beat RMSE/SSIM/PSNR), not to HFEN being tested there.

sub_claims.text: The Mason 2020 study was performed on natural images.

evidence.snippet: Title: 'Comparison of Objective Image Quality Metrics to Expert Radiologists' Scoring of Diagnostic Quality of MR Images'

The comparison uses a database of MR images of the brain and abdomen that have been retrospectively degraded by noise, blurring, undersampling, motion, and wavelet compression for a total of 414 degraded images.

The quality of a magnetic resonance (MR) image can be difficult to assess in a robust, objective and quantitative manner... In practice, clinical MR images are typically viewed by an expert radiologist, so the radiologist's opinion of the diagnostic quality of the image can then be considered an appropriate measure of MR image quality.

Some previous studies that have attempted to quantify performance of common full reference objective IQMs for MR images have used non-expert raters... For many non-medical IQM studies (for example, rating the quality of a television picture) the use of non-expert raters is sufficient... This is not the case for medical images because medical images are designed to be viewed by expert radiologists.

Our current work focused on clinically normal images, a critical first step in determining if IQMs developed for non-experts rating natural images would also correlate with radiologists rating diagnostic quality of MR images.

sub_claims.nuance: Mason 2020 is explicitly a study of MR images (brain and body/abdomen), scored by expert radiologists. In fact, Mason's own Discussion contrasts its MR-image scope against prior IQMs 'developed for non-experts rating natural images' — the opposite framing of the manuscript's characterization. The manuscript's 'natural images' descriptor is a factual error; the correct descriptor is 'MR images' or 'medical images'.

remediation.suggested_edit: Replace 'similar to the findings of the Mason study6 of natural images' with 'similar to the findings of the Mason study6 of MR images' (or 'of medical images'). Mason 2020 evaluated objective IQMs against expert radiologist scoring of MR images of the brain and abdomen — not natural images. The outperform pattern (VIF/FSIM/NQM/GMSD/IWSSIM/HDRVDP beating RMSE/SSIM/PSNR) is correctly attributed; only the 'natural images' descriptor is wrong.

phrasings_tried: outperform NRMSE / SSIM / PSNR in correlation with radiologist / reader scores
HFEN tested or evaluated in Mason study
natural images vs MR images (study subject matter)
SROCC / Spearman correlation between IQM and radiologist scores
which IQMs beat RMSE/SSIM — VIF, FSIM, NQM, GMSD, IWSSIM, HDRVDP
attestation.closest_adjacent: Mason Discussion L12: 'Our current work focused on clinically normal images, a critical first step in determining if IQMs developed for non-experts rating natural images would also correlate with radiologists rating diagnostic quality of MR images.' — Mason explicitly distinguishes its MR-image scope from the 'natural images' IQM literature it draws on. This is the nearest passage to the manuscript's 'natural images' framing, and it directly contradicts that framing (Mason's target was MR, and it contrasts itself against natural-image IQM studies).
attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Mason 2020 is the primary source for its findings on RMSE/SSIM/PSNR vs. higher-performing IQMs correlating with radiologist scores on MR images — not an indirect citation. Mason cites refs [16], [17], [20] as related MR-IQM work using non-expert raters or only SSIM-family metrics, but Mason itself conducts the study the manuscript references.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The outperform finding (sub-claim a) is used in-context: the manuscript cites Mason to support that higher-quality IQMs beat RMSE/SSIM/PSNR in reader-correlation, which is exactly Mason's headline result. However, the 'natural images' descriptor applied to Mason (sub-claim b) is out-of-context: Mason studied MR images of brain and abdomen, scored by five expert radiologists — the 'natural images' label is factually incorrect, not merely a paraphrase.
